name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
ٱذْهَبُوا۟ بِقَمِيصِى هَـٰذَا فَأَلْقُوهُ عَلَىٰ وَجْهِ أَبِى يَأْتِ بَصِيرًا وَأْتُونِى بِأَهْلِكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٩٣)
```

```english
Take this shirt of mine, and cast it upon my father’s face; he will regain his sight, and bring me all your folks.’ (93)
```

```arabic
وَلَمَّا فَصَلَتِ ٱلْعِيرُ قَالَ أَبُوهُمْ إِنِّى لَأَجِدُ رِيحَ يُوسُفَ ۖ لَوْلَآ أَن تُفَنِّدُونِ (٩٤)
```

```english
As the caravan set off, their father said, ‘I sense the scent of Joseph, if you will not consider me a dotard.’ (94)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ تَٱللَّهِ إِنَّكَ لَفِى ضَلَـٰلِكَ ٱلْقَدِيمِ (٩٥)
```

```english
They said, ‘By God, you persist in your inveterate error.’ (95)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّآ أَن جَآءَ ٱلْبَشِيرُ أَلْقَىٰهُ عَلَىٰ وَجْهِهِۦ فَٱرْتَدَّ بَصِيرًا ۖ قَالَ أَلَمْ أَقُل لَّكُمْ إِنِّىٓ أَعْلَمُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٩٦)
```

```english
When the bearer of good news arrived, he cast it on his face, and he regained his sight. He said, ‘Did I not tell you, ‘‘I know from Allah what you do not know?’’ ’ (96)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَبَانَا ٱسْتَغْفِرْ لَنَا ذُنُوبَنَآ إِنَّا كُنَّا خَـٰطِـِٔينَ (٩٧)
```

```english
They said, ‘Father! Plead [with Allah] for forgiveness of our sins! We have indeed been erring.’ (97)
```

```arabic
قَالَ سَوْفَ أَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكُمْ رَبِّىٓ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٩٨)
```

```english
He said, ‘I shall plead with my Lord to forgive you; indeed He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful.’ (98)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّا دَخَلُوا۟ عَلَىٰ يُوسُفَ ءَاوَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِ أَبَوَيْهِ وَقَالَ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ مِصْرَ إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ءَامِنِينَ (٩٩)
```

```english
When they entered into the presence of Joseph, he set his parents close to himself, and said, ‘Welcome to Egypt, in safety, God willing!’ (99)
```

```arabic
وَرَفَعَ أَبَوَيْهِ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ وَخَرُّوا۟ لَهُۥ سُجَّدًا ۖ وَقَالَ يَـٰٓأَبَتِ هَـٰذَا تَأْوِيلُ رُءْيَـٰىَ مِن قَبْلُ قَدْ جَعَلَهَا رَبِّى حَقًّا ۖ وَقَدْ أَحْسَنَ بِىٓ إِذْ أَخْرَجَنِى مِنَ ٱلسِّجْنِ وَجَآءَ بِكُم مِّنَ ٱلْبَدْوِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ أَن نَّزَغَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَ إِخْوَتِىٓ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى لَطِيفٌ لِّمَا يَشَآءُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْعَلِيمُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (١٠٠)
```

```english
And he seated his parents high upon the throne, and they fell down prostrate before him. He said, ‘Father! This is the fulfillment of my dream of long ago, which my Lord has made come true. He was certainly gracious to me when He brought me out of the prison and brought you over from the desert after that Satan had incited ill feeling between me and my brothers. Indeed my Lord is all-attentive in bringing about what He wishes. Indeed He is the All-knowing, the All-wise.’ (100)
```

```arabic
۞ رَبِّ قَدْ ءَاتَيْتَنِى مِنَ ٱلْمُلْكِ وَعَلَّمْتَنِى مِن تَأْوِيلِ ٱلْأَحَادِيثِ ۚ فَاطِرَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ أَنتَ وَلِىِّۦ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ تَوَفَّنِى مُسْلِمًا وَأَلْحِقْنِى بِٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (١٠١)
```

```english
‘My Lord! You have granted me a share in the kingdom, and taught me the interpretation of dreams. Originator of the heavens and earth! You are my guardian in this world and the Hereafter! Let my death be in submission [to You], and unite me with the Righteous.’ (101)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآءِ ٱلْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهِ إِلَيْكَ ۖ وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ أَجْمَعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَهُمْ وَهُمْ يَمْكُرُونَ (١٠٢)
```

```english
These are accounts from the Unseen, which We reveal to you, and you were not with them when they conspired together and schemed. (102)
```

## (Commentary)

This marks the conclusion of the story of Yusuf (peace be upon him). The verses encompass Yusuf’s command to his brothers to carry his shirt to his father, their arrival to him with all their families, and subsequently their entry into Egypt and his meeting with his parents.

**His saying, the Exalted:** اذهبوا بقميصي هذا فألقوه على وجه أبي يأت بصيرا وأتوني بأهلكم أجمعين **"Take this shirt of mine and cast it upon my father’s face; he will regain his sight. And bring me all your folks." (12:93)** This is the completion of the speech of Yusuf (peace be upon him), in which he commands his brothers to take his shirt to his father and cast it upon his face so that Allah may heal his eyes through it, and he may regain his sight (يأت بصيرا) after having become blind (ضريرا) due to excessive grief and weeping.

This is the last of the wondrous cares (العنايات البديعة) that Allah, the Glorified, manifested for Yusuf (peace be upon him), as recounted in this Surah. It demonstrates how Allah overcame the secondary causes (الأسباب) and turned them in a direction contrary to where they were heading. His brothers envied him, humiliated him, and estranged him from his home by casting him into the pit (الجب) and selling him to the caravan for a paltry price. Yet, Allah, the Glorified, made this very cause the means for his settlement in the house of the *Aziz* of Egypt in an honorable abode. Then, He established him upon the throne of might (أريكة عزة), before which his brothers pleaded, saying: يا أيها العزيز مسنا وأهلنا الضر وجئنا ببضاعة مزجاة فأوف لنا الكيل وتصدق علينا إن الله يجزي المتصدقين **"O *Aziz*! Distress has visited us and our family, and we have brought a meager sum. So dispense the full measure to us and be charitable to us. Indeed Allah rewards the charitable." (12:88)**

Furthermore, the wife of the *Aziz* and the women of Egypt loved him and enticed him (راودنه) against his soul to lead him into the destruction of immorality. But Allah protected him and made that the cause for the manifestation of his innocence and the perfection of his chastity (عفته). Then they humiliated him and imprisoned him, yet Allah made that the cause for his might and sovereignty.

His brothers had come to his father on the day they cast him into the depths of the pit with his shirt stained with blood, falsely informing him of his death. Thus, the shirt was the cause of his father’s grief and weeping over the separation from his son until his eyes turned white and his sight departed. Now, Allah, the Glorified, restored his sight to him through that very shirt. In short, the causes conspired to lower him, but Allah, the Glorified, willed to raise him. Thus, what Allah willed came to pass, rather than that toward which the causes were directed. And Allah is master over His affair (والله غالب على أمره).

His saying: وأتوني بأهلكم أجمعين **"And bring me all your folks"** is a command from him for the relocation of the House of Ya’qub—including Ya’qub, his family, his sons, and all their descendants—from the desert (البدو) to Egypt to settle there.

**His saying, the Exalted:** ولما فصلت العير قال أبوهم إني لأجد ريح يوسف لو لا أن تفندون **"As the caravan departed, their father said, ‘I feel the scent of Yusuf, if you will not think me doting.’" (12:94)** Departure (الفصل) means cutting off or breaking away. Doting (التفنيد) is the verbal noun of *al-fanad*, which refers to weakness of judgment (ضعف الرأي). The meaning is: when the caravan carrying Yusuf’s shirt left Egypt and broke away from it, their father Ya’qub said to those of his sons present with him: "I truly perceive the scent of Yusuf, were it not that you would accuse me of weakness of judgment." That is: "I sense his scent and see that the meeting is near, and it is only right that you submit to what I perceive, were it not that you would find me in error; however, it is likely that you will dismiss me as doting and not submit to my word."

**His saying, the Exalted:** قالوا تالله إنك لفي ضلالك القديم **"They said, ‘By Allah, you are indeed in your old error.’" (12:95)** The "old" (القديم) is the opposite of the "new," referring to that which preceded in existence. This is what some of his sons present with him confronted him with. It was part of their ill fortune in this story that they uttered similar words at the beginning of the story when they said: إن أبانا لفي ضلال مبين **"Indeed our father is in manifest error" (12:8)** and at its conclusion, which is this saying of theirs: تالله إنك لفي ضلالك القديم **"By Allah, you are indeed in your old error."**

It appears that their meaning of "error" (الضلال) here is the same as their meaning of "error" there: namely, his excessive love for Yusuf. This was because they believed they were more entitled to love than Yusuf, as they were a cohesive group (عصبة) responsible for managing his household and defending him. Yet, they felt their father had strayed from the level path of wisdom (طريق الحكمة) by preferring over them two small children who could avail him nothing, turning entirely toward them and forgetting the others. Then, when Yusuf was lost, he grieved for him and did not cease grieving and weeping until his eyes failed and his back became bowed.

This is what they meant by him being in his "old error"; they did not mean error in religion (الضلال في الدين), which would have rendered them disbelievers (كافرين).

Firstly: because the various parts of their speech throughout the story testify that they were monotheists (موحدين) following the religion of their fathers Ibrahim, Ishaq, and Ya’qub (peace be upon them).

Secondly: because the context here, as well as at the beginning of the story when they said: إن أبانا لفي ضلال مبين **"Indeed our father is in manifest error,"** has no connection to error in religion such that they could be suspected of accusing their father of it. Rather, it concerns a practical, life-related matter: a father’s love for some of his children and his preference for them in honor over others. This is what they meant by "error."

**His saying, the Exalted:** فلما أن جاء البشير ألقاه على وجهه فارتد بصيرا قال ألم أقل لكم إني أعلم من الله ما لا تعلمون **"When the bearer of good news arrived, he cast it upon his face, and he regained his sight. He said, ‘Did I not tell you, "I know from Allah what you do not know"?’" (12:96)** The bearer of good news (البشير) was the one carrying the shirt. His saying: ألم أقل لكم إني أعلم **"Did I not tell you, ‘I know...’"** refers to his (peace be upon him) statement to them when they blamed him for mentioning Yusuf: إنما أشكو بثي وحزني إلى الله وأعلم من الله ما لا تعلمون **"I only complain of my grief and sorrow to Allah, and I know from Allah what you do not know." (12:86)** The meaning of the verse is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قالوا يا أبانا استغفر لنا ذنوبنا إنا كنا خاطئين **"They said, ‘O father! Plead [with Allah] for the forgiveness of our sins! Indeed we have been erring.’" (12:97)** The speakers are the sons of Ya’qub, as evidenced by their saying: يا أبانا **"O father!"** By "sins" (الذنوب), they mean what they did regarding Yusuf and his brother. As for Yusuf, he had already sought forgiveness for them previously.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قال سوف أستغفر لكم ربي إنه هو الغفور الرحيم **"He said, ‘I shall plead with my Lord to forgive you. Indeed He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful.’" (12:98)** He (peace be upon him) delayed seeking forgiveness for them, as indicated by his saying: سوف أستغفر لكم ربي **"I shall plead with my Lord to forgive you."** Perhaps he delayed it so that the blessing might be completed by the meeting with Yusuf, and his soul might be fully comforted by him, forgetting all traces of the separation, before he sought forgiveness for them. In some narrations, it is mentioned that he delayed it until a time when supplication is answered (يستجاب فيه الدعاء), which will be discussed, Allah willing.

**His saying, the Exalted:** فلما دخلوا على يوسف آوى إليه أبويه وقال ادخلوا مصر إن شاء الله آمنين **"When they entered into the presence of Yusuf, he gave a place to his parents with himself and said, ‘Enter Egypt, if Allah wills, in security.’" (12:99)** There is an omission in the speech, the estimation of which is: "So Ya’qub and his family left their land and traveled to Egypt, and when they entered..." etc.

His saying: آوى إليه أبويه **"he gave a place to his parents with himself"** has been interpreted as him drawing them close to him. His saying: وقال ادخلوا مصر **"and said, ‘Enter Egypt...’"** etc., clearly indicates that Yusuf went out of Egypt to receive them and drew them to himself there, then offered for them to enter Egypt out of honor and etiquette. He (peace be upon him) spoke exquisitely in saying: إن شاء الله آمنين **"if Allah wills, in security,"** whereby he granted them security and issued his decree for them according to the custom of kings, yet conditioned it upon the will of Allah, the Glorified. This indicates that the human will (المشية الإنسانية) does not produce its effect, like all other causes, unless it accords with the Divine Will (المشية الإلهية), as necessitated by pure monotheism (التوحيد الخالص). The apparent sense of this context is that they could not enter and settle in Egypt except by permission from the King; hence, he granted them security at the very outset.

Allah, the Glorified, mentioned "his parents" (أبويه), and the commentators differ as to whether they were his actual father and mother, or Ya’qub and his wife, who was Yusuf’s maternal aunt, based on the premise that his mother died while he was young. There is nothing in the speech of the Exalted that supports either possibility, except that the apparent meaning of "parents" (الأبوين) refers to the biological ones.

The meaning of the verse is: "When they entered"—meaning his parents, his brothers, and their families—"into the presence of Yusuf," which was outside of Egypt, "he gave a place" and drew "to himself his parents and said" to them, granting them security: "Enter Egypt, if Allah wills, in security."

**His saying, the Exalted:** ورفع أبويه على العرش وخروا له سجدا وقال يا أبت هذا تأويل رؤياي **"And he seated his parents high upon the throne, and they fell down prostrate before him. He said, ‘O father! This is the fulfillment of my dream...’" (12:100)** to the end of the verse. The throne (العرش) is the high seat, often used for that upon which a king sits and which is exclusive to him. Falling down (الخرور) is dropping to the ground. The desert (البدو) is the wilderness, for Ya’qub used to dwell in the desert.

His saying: ورفع أبويه على العرش **"And he seated his parents high upon the throne"** means Yusuf raised his parents onto the royal throne upon which he used to sit. Reason and the apparent context suggest they were raised onto the throne by Yusuf’s command, carried out by his servants, rather than by him personally, as hinted by the saying: وخروا له سجدا **"and they fell down prostrate before him."** For it appears that the prostration occurred the moment Yusuf first appeared to them. It was as if they had entered the house and settled in their seats, then Yusuf entered upon them, and the Divine light (النور الإلهي) shimmering from his wondrous beauty overwhelmed them, so they could not restrain themselves from falling down in prostration to him.

His saying: وخروا له سجدا **"and they fell down prostrate before him"**—the pronoun refers to Yusuf, as the context implies; thus, he was the one prostrated to. The claim of some that the pronoun refers to Allah, the Glorified, based on the impermissibility of prostrating to other than Allah, has no evidence from the wording. A similar instance occurred in the Holy Quran in the story of Adam and the angels, where the Exalted said: وإذ قلنا للملائكة اسجدوا لآدم فسجدوا إلا إبليس **"And when We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam,’ they prostrated, but not Iblis." (20:116)**

The proof that this was not a prostration of worship (سجدة عبادة) to Yusuf is that among those prostrating was Ya’qub (peace be upon him), who is among those the Holy Quran explicitly states is purified (مخلص) for Allah, associating nothing with Him. Yusuf (peace be upon him)—the one prostrated to—is also among them by the text of the Quran, and he is the one who said to his two companions in prison: "It is not for us to associate anything with Allah," and he did not restrain them.

Thus, it is only that they took Yusuf as a sign (آية) of Allah, making him a *qiblah* in their prostration, thereby worshipping Allah alone. This is like the Kaaba, which is taken as a *qiblah* to be prayed toward; through it, Allah is worshipped, not the Kaaba itself. It is well known that a sign, insofar as it is a sign, has no independent reality (نفسية) at all; thus, the one worshipped in its presence is none but Allah, the Glorified and Exalted. This concept has been discussed repeatedly in previous parts of this book.

From this, it becomes clear that the justifications mentioned by some—such as the claim that the greeting of people in those days was prostration, just as in Islam it is the *Salam*; or that the custom of the great ones then was prostration and it had not yet been forbidden for other than Allah as it was in Islam; or that their prostration was in the form of bowing (ركوع) as the non-Arabs do—all of these are untenable.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قال يا أبت هذا تأويل رؤياي من قبل قد جعلها ربي حقا **"He said, ‘O father! This is the fulfillment of my dream of long ago, which my Lord has made come true...’" (12:100)** to the end of the verse. When he (peace be upon him) witnessed the prostration of his parents and his eleven brothers, he remembered the dream in which he saw eleven stars and the sun and the moon prostrating to him. He had told his father of it when he was young, and his father had interpreted it for him. So he pointed to their prostration to him and said: "O father! This is the fulfillment of my dream of long ago; my Lord has made it"—meaning the dream—"come true."

Then he praised his Lord in gratitude, saying: وقد أحسن بي إذ أخرجني من السجن **"He was certainly kind to me when He brought me out of the prison" (12:100)**. He mentioned his Lord’s kindness to him in bringing him out of prison, which was a state of distress and trial (ضراء وبلاء) that Allah removed from him, replacing it with ease and blessing (سراء ونعمة) from where he did not expect, making it the means for his attainment of might and sovereignty.

He did not mention his removal from the pit (الجب) before that, out of consideration for his brothers’ presence; he did not wish to mention what would distress them, out of nobility and chivalry (كرما وفتوة). Rather, he alluded to it with the most beautiful words possible, without containing any reproach or animosity toward them, saying: وجاء بكم من البدو من بعد أن نزغ الشيطان بيني وبين إخوتي **"and brought you from the desert after Satan had caused a rift between me and my brothers." (12:100)** A rift (النزغ) is entering into a matter to corrupt it.

The meaning is: "He was certainly kind to me after Satan sowed discord between me and my brothers, and what happened happened, leading to the separation between me and you. Then my Lord led me to Egypt, established me in the most affluent life and the highest might and sovereignty, then brought us close by relocating you from the desert to me in the abode of urbanity and civilization."

He meant that there were calamities that befell him following Satan’s corruption between him and his brothers, and among those he specifically mentions are the separation between him and them, and then the tragedy of the prison. His Lord was kind to him and removed them from him one after another. These were not ordinary trials or events, but deafening tragedies and unbreakable knots; yet his Lord penetrated them with His grace (لطفه) and the efficacy of His power, transforming them into causes of life and blessing after they had been causes of destruction and misery. For these last three points, he followed his saying: وقد أحسن بي **"He was certainly kind to me..."** etc., with his saying: إن ربي لطيف لما يشاء **"Indeed my Lord is kind to whomever He wishes." (12:100)**

So his saying: إن ربي لطيف لما يشاء **"Indeed my Lord is kind to whomever He wishes"** is the justification for his release from prison and their arrival from the desert. He points by it to the special care and favor Allah bestowed upon him, and that the trials surrounding him were not such that their knots could be untied or their course diverted; but Allah is kind to what He wills—He penetrated them and made the factors of hardship into factors of ease and comfort, and the causes of humiliation and slavery into means of might and sovereignty.

The Kind (اللطيف) is one of His names, the Exalted, indicating His presence and His encompassment (إحاطته) of that which there is no way to be present in or encompass from the interior of things. It is a branch of His encompassment through the efficacy of power and knowledge. The Exalted said: ألا يعلم من خلق وهو اللطيف الخبير **"Does He who created not know? And He is the All-attentive, the All-aware." (67:14)** The root of its meaning is smallness, subtlety, and penetration. It is said: *latufa al-shay’* (لطف الشيء) when it becomes small and subtle until it penetrates through narrow channels and holes. It is used metaphorically for kindness and compatibility, and the noun is *al-lutf* (اللطف).

His saying: هو العليم الحكيم **"He is the All-knowing, the All-wise"** is the justification for all that preceded from his saying: يا أبت هذا تأويل رؤياي من قبل قد جعلها ربي حقا **"O father! This is the fulfillment of my dream of long ago, which my Lord has made come true..."** etc. He (peace be upon him) justified the speech and concluded it with these two names in alignment with his father, who had spoken regarding his dream and said: وكذلك يجتبيك ربك **"That is how your Lord will choose you..."** until he said: إن ربك عليم حكيم **"Indeed your Lord is All-knowing, All-wise." (12:6)** It is not unlikely that the definite article (ال) in his saying: العليم الحكيم **"the All-knowing, the All-wise"** conveys the meaning of reference (العهد), thereby signifying his confirmation of his father’s (peace be upon him) words. The meaning would be: "He is that very All-knowing, All-wise One whom you described to me on the day you interpreted my dream."

**His saying, the Exalted:** رب قد آتيتني من الملك وعلمتني من تأويل الأحاديث **"My Lord! You have given me a share of sovereignty and taught me the interpretation of dreams..." (12:101)** to the end of the verse. When he (peace be upon him) had praised his Lord and enumerated the hardships and calamities He had averted from him, he wished to mention the established blessings He had specifically granted him. Divine love (المحبة الإلهية) had been stirred within him, causing him to detach from everything other than Him, the Exalted. So he left off addressing his father and turned away from him and everyone else, turning toward his Lord, and addressed his Lord, exalted is His Name, saying: "My Lord! You have given me a share of sovereignty and taught me the interpretation of dreams."

His saying: فاطر السماوات والأرض أنت وليي في الدنيا والآخرة **"Originator of the heavens and the earth! You are my guardian in this world and the Hereafter" (12:101)** is a transition and an escalation in praise. It is a return by him (peace be upon him) to the mention of the origin of Divine guardianship (الولاية الإلهية) after having mentioned some of its manifest expressions, such as his release from prison, the arrival of his family from the desert, the granting of sovereignty, and the teaching of the interpretation of dreams. For Allah, the Glorified, is the Lord in both the subtle and the grand; He is the Guardian (ولي) in both this world and the Hereafter.

His guardianship (ولايته), the Exalted—meaning His being the sustainer of every thing in its essence, attributes, and acts—originates from His bringing them all into existence and manifesting them from the concealment of non-existence. Thus, He is the Originator of the heavens and the earth (فاطر السماوات والأرض). Therefore, the hearts of His friends (أولياء) and His purified servants turn toward Him through this name, which signifies His existence by His essence and His bringing others into existence. The Exalted said: قالت رسلهم أفي الله شك فاطر السماوات والأرض **"Their apostles said, ‘Is there any doubt about Allah, the Originator of the heavens and the earth?’" (14:10)**

For this reason, Yusuf (peace be upon him)—being among the purified (المخلصين)—began with it in mentioning His guardianship, saying: فاطر السماوات والأرض أنت وليي في الدنيا والآخرة **"Originator of the heavens and the earth! You are my guardian in this world and the Hereafter."** That is: "I am under Your complete guardianship, without having any hand in my own making or any independence in my essence, attributes, or acts; nor do I possess for myself any benefit, harm, death, life, or resurrection."

His saying: توفني مسلما وألحقني بالصالحين **"Let my death be in submission [to You], and join me with the righteous." (12:101)** When he (peace be upon him) became immersed in the station of humility before the Lord of Might and witnessed His guardianship over him in this world and the Hereafter, he asked Him—as a slave asks the master who has authority over him—to make him as His guardianship over him in this world and the Hereafter requires. This is submission (الإسلام) as long as he is alive in this world, and entry into the company of the righteous (الصالحين) in the Hereafter. For the perfection of the enslaved servant is to submit (يسلم) to his Lord what He wants from him as long as he is alive, such that nothing he does of his own choice is disliked or disapproved by Him; and that he be righteous (صالحا) for the proximity of his Master, worthy of His lofty gifts in matters that do not return to the servant and his choice. This is his (peace be upon him) request for submission in this world and entry into the company of the righteous in the Hereafter. This is what Allah, the Glorified, granted to his grandfather Ibrahim (peace be upon him): ولقد اصطفيناه في الدنيا وإنه في الآخرة لمن الصالحين إذ قال له ربه أسلم قال أسلمت لرب العالمين **"Certainly We chose him in the present world, and in the Hereafter he will indeed be among the righteous. When his Lord said to him, ‘Submit,’ he said, ‘I submit to the Lord of all the worlds.’" (2:130-131)**

This submission (الإسلام) which he (peace be upon him) requested is the highest degree of submission and its loftiest rank. It is pure surrender (التسليم المحض) to Allah, the Glorified, wherein the servant sees no independence for himself or for the effects of his self, such that nothing of his self, its attributes, or its acts distracts him from his Lord. When attributed to Him, the Exalted, it is His purifying (إخلاص) His servant for Himself.

From what has preceded, it becomes clear that his saying: توفني مسلما **"Let my death be in submission [to You]"** is a request for the permanence of sincerity (الإخلاص) and the continuation of submission as long as he is alive. In other words, that he may live as a submitter (مسلما) until Allah takes his soul. It is a metaphor for Allah keeping him firm upon submission until he dies. It does not mean a request to die in a state of submission even if he were not a submitter before that, nor is it a request for death while he is a submitter such that the meaning would be "I am a submitter, so take my soul."

This clarifies the error of what has been narrated from several early commentators that his saying: توفني مسلما **"Let my death be in submission [to You]"** is a prayer by which he asks for death from Allah, the Glorified, to the point that some said: "No prophet ever asked for death from Allah or longed for it except Yusuf (peace be upon him)."

**His saying, the Exalted:** ذلك من أنباء الغيب نوحيه إليك وما كنت لديهم إذ أجمعوا أمرهم وهم يمكرون **"These are from the accounts of the Unseen (الغيب) — *that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge* — which We reveal to you. You were not with them when they conspired and as they were plotting." (12:102)** The reference is to the account of Yusuf (peace be upon him). The address is to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). The plural pronoun refers to Yusuf’s brothers. Conspiring (الإجماع) is resolve and intent.

His saying: وما كنت لديهم **"You were not with them..."** etc., is a circumstantial clause (حال) for the pronoun of address in "to you" (إليك). His saying: نوحيه إليك وما كنت **"which We reveal to you. You were not..."** to the end of the verse is an explanation of His saying: ذلك من أنباء الغيب **"These are from the accounts of the Unseen."** The meaning is that the account of Yusuf is from the accounts of the Unseen, for We reveal it to you while you were not present with Yusuf’s brothers when they resolved upon their matter and were plotting regarding Yusuf.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, narrated from Abu Basir from Abu Ja’far (peace be upon him) in a long tradition, he said: Yusuf said to his brothers: لا تثريب عليكم اليوم يغفر الله لكم اذهبوا بقميصي هذا **"There shall be no reproach on you today. Allah will forgive you... Take this shirt of mine"** which has been moistened by the tears of my eyes, فألقوه على وجه أبي يأت بصيرا **"and cast it upon my father’s face; he will regain his sight"**—as soon as my scent spreads— وأتوني بأهلكم أجمعين **"and bring me all your folks."** He sent them back to Ya’qub on that day and equipped them with everything they needed. When their caravan departed from Egypt, Ya’qub perceived the scent of Yusuf and said to those of his children present with him: "I truly perceive the scent of Yusuf, were it not that you would think me doting."

He said: His children approached, hastening their pace with the shirt in joy and happiness—at what they saw of Yusuf’s state, the sovereignty Allah had given him, and the might they attained under Yusuf’s authority. Their journey from Egypt to Ya’qub’s land was nine days. When the bearer of good news arrived, he cast the shirt upon his face and he regained his sight. He said to them: "What did Binyamin do?" They said: "We left him with his brother, well."

He said: At that, Ya’qub praised Allah and performed a prostration of gratitude (سجدة الشكر) to his Lord. His sight returned to him and his back straightened. He said to his children: "Travel to Yusuf this very day, all of you." So they traveled to Yusuf, and with them were Ya’qub and Yusuf’s maternal aunt, "Yamil." They hastened their pace in joy and happiness, traveling for nine days to Egypt.

**I say:** The fact that Ya’qub’s wife who traveled with him to Egypt—the mother of Binyamin—was Yusuf’s maternal aunt and not his biological mother occurs in several narrations. The apparent sense of the Book and some narrations is that she was Yusuf’s mother, and that he and Binyamin were full brothers from the same mother, though this is not an apparent sense that would dismiss those narrations.

In *al-Majma’*, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: ولما فصلت العير قال أبوهم إني لأجد ريح يوسف لو لا أن تفندون **"As the caravan departed, their father said, ‘I feel the scent of Yusuf, if you will not think me doting.’"** He said: Ya’qub perceived the scent of Yusuf when it departed from Egypt—while he was in Palestine, a distance of ten nights’ journey.

**I say:** It has been mentioned in several narrations from both Sunni and Shi’a sources that the shirt Yusuf sent to Ya’qub (peace be upon him) had descended from Paradise. It was the shirt of Ibrahim, which Jibril brought down to him when he was cast into the fire, and he clothed him in it, so it became coolness and peace for him. Then Ishaq inherited it, then Ya’qub inherited it, then Ya’qub made it an amulet (تميمة) and hung it on Yusuf when he was born. It remained on his neck until Yusuf took it out of the amulet, and the scent of Paradise wafted from it, which Ya’qub perceived. These are reports for which we have no way of verifying, in addition to the weakness of their chains of transmission.

Similar to them are other narrations from both groups containing a letter Ya’qub wrote to Yusuf—while he thought him to be the *Aziz* of the House of Pharaoh—to secure the release of Binyamin, mentioning that he is the son of Ishaq, the sacrifice (ذبيح) of Allah, whom Allah commanded his grandfather Ibrahim to sacrifice and then ransomed with a great sacrifice. It has already been mentioned in the previous part of the book that the sacrifice was Isma’il, not Ishaq.

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Nashit ibn Nasih al-Bajali, he said: I said to Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): "Were Yusuf’s brothers prophets?" He said: "No, nor were they righteous and God-conscious (بررة أتقياء). How could they be, when they said to their father: تالله إنك لفي ضلالك القديم **'By Allah, you are indeed in your old error'**?"

**I say:** In narrations from Sunni sources and in some weak Shi’a narrations, it is claimed they were prophets. These narrations are refuted by what is established through the Book, the Sunnah, and reason regarding the infallibility (عصمة) of the prophets (peace be upon them). What appears in the Book suggesting the Tribes (الأسباط) were prophets—such as the saying of the Exalted: وأوحينا إلى إبراهيم وإسماعيل وإسحاق ويعقوب والأسباط **"and We revealed to Ibrahim, Isma’il, Ishaq, Ya’qub, and the Tribes" (4:163)**—is not explicit that the "Tribes" refers to Yusuf’s brothers. The term "Tribes" is applied to all the peoples of the Children of Israel whose lineage traces back to Ya’qub (peace be upon him). The Exalted said: وقطعناهم اثنتي عشرة أسباطا أمما **"We divided them into twelve tribes, as nations." (7:160)**

In *al-Faqih*, with its chain from Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): regarding Ya’qub’s saying to his sons: سوف أستغفر لكم ربي **"I shall plead with my Lord to forgive you,"** he said: "He delayed them until the dawn (السحر) of Friday night."

**I say:** There are other narrations to this effect. In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, from Ibn Jarir and Abu al-Shaykh from Ibn ‘Abbas from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), he said: "The saying of my brother Ya’qub to his sons: سوف أستغفر لكم ربي **'I shall plead with my Lord to forgive you'** means: until Friday night comes."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain from al-Fadl ibn Abi Qurrah from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "The best time you can supplicate to Allah is at dawn (الأسحار)," and he recited this verse regarding Ya’qub’s saying: سوف أستغفر لكم ربي **"I shall plead with my Lord to forgive you"**—he delayed them until dawn.

**I say:** A similar report is narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Abu al-Shaykh and Ibn Mardawayh from Ibn ‘Abbas: that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was asked why Ya’qub delayed his sons in seeking forgiveness. He said: "He delayed them until dawn because the supplication at dawn is answered."

The explanation of the verses already discussed the reason for the delay. Yusuf (peace be upon him) turned toward his brothers with chivalry and nobility as soon as they recognized him, without confronting them with the slightest thing that would distress them; this necessitated that he forgive them and seek forgiveness for them without delay. Ya’qub’s position with them when his sight returned by the casting of the shirt was not that same position.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, Muhammad ibn ‘Isa narrated to me: that Yahya ibn Aktham asked Musa ibn Muhammad ibn ‘Ali ibn Musa several questions, which he presented to Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him). One of them was: "Inform me about the saying of Allah: ورفع أبويه على العرش وخروا له سجدا **'And he seated his parents high upon the throne, and they fell down prostrate before him.'** Did Ya’qub and his children prostrate to Yusuf while they were prophets?"

Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him) replied: "As for the prostration of Ya’qub and his children to Yusuf, it was not for Yusuf. Rather, it was from Ya’qub and his children an act of obedience to Allah and a greeting (تحية) to Yusuf—just as the prostration of the angels was for Adam, yet it was not for Adam; rather, it was from them an act of obedience to Allah and a greeting to Adam. So Ya’qub, his children, and Yusuf with them prostrated in gratitude to Allah, the Exalted, for the reuniting of their family. Do you not see that he says in his gratitude at that time: رب قد آتيتني من الملك وعلمتني من تأويل الأحاديث فاطر السماوات والأرض أنت وليي في الدنيا والآخرة توفني مسلما وألحقني بالصالحين **'My Lord! You have given me a share of sovereignty and taught me the interpretation of dreams. Originator of the heavens and the earth! You are my guardian in this world and the Hereafter. Let my death be in submission [to You], and join me with the righteous'**?" (The tradition continues).

**I say:** Some discussion regarding their prostration to Yusuf has already preceded in the explanation of the verses. The apparent sense of the tradition is that Yusuf also prostrated with them as they prostrated, and this was inferred from Yusuf’s saying in his gratitude: رب قد آتيتني من الملك **"My Lord! You have given me a share of sovereignty..."** etc. However, there is some ambiguity in its indication of that.

Al-Ayyashi narrated the tradition in his *Tafsir* from Muhammad ibn Sa’id al-Azdi, the companion of Musa ibn Muhammad ibn al-Rida (peace be upon him): He said to his brother: "Yahya ibn Aktham wrote to him asking about several matters... so inform me about the saying of Allah: ورفع أبويه على العرش وخروا له سجدا **'And he seated his parents high upon the throne, and they fell down prostrate before him.'** Did Ya’qub and his children prostrate to Yusuf?"

He said: "I asked my brother about that, and he said: 'As for the prostration of Ya’qub and his children to Yusuf, it was in gratitude to Allah, the Exalted, for the reuniting of their family. Do you not see that he says in gratitude at that time: رب قد آتيتني من الملك وعلمتني من تأويل الأحاديث **"My Lord! You have given me a share of sovereignty and taught me the interpretation of dreams..."** the verse.'"

What al-Ayyashi narrated is more in accord with the wording of the verse and safer from difficulty than what al-Qummi narrated.

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr from some of our companions from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of Allah: ورفع أبويه على العرش **"And he seated his parents high upon the throne,"** he said: "The throne (العرش) is the seat (السرير)." And regarding His saying: وخروا له سجدا **"and they fell down prostrate before him,"** he said: "That prostration of theirs was worship (عبادة) to Allah."

In the same source, from Abu Basir from Abu Ja’far (peace be upon him) in a tradition, he said: "He traveled for nine days to Egypt. When they entered upon Yusuf in the royal palace, he embraced his father, kissed him, and wept. He raised his maternal aunt onto the royal seat. Then he entered his house, anointed himself, applied kohl, and wore the garments of might and sovereignty. Then he returned to them—and in one version: then he went out to them—and when they saw him, they all prostrated in veneration and gratitude to Allah. At that, he said: يا أبت هذا تأويل رؤياي من قبل **'O father! This is the fulfillment of my dream of long ago...'** to his saying: بيني وبين إخوتي **'...between me and my brothers.'**"

He said: "During those twenty years, Yusuf did not anoint himself, nor apply kohl, nor use perfume, nor laugh, nor touch women, until Allah reunited Ya’qub’s family and brought him together with Ya’qub and his brothers."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain from al-‘Abbas ibn Hilal al-Shami, the client of Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him), from him, he said: I said to him: "May I be your ransom! What is most pleasing to people is one who eats coarse food, wears rough clothing, and is humble." He said: "Do you not know that Yusuf was a prophet, the son of a prophet? He used to wear robes of brocade (ديباج) fastened with gold, and he would sit in the assemblies of the House of Pharaoh and judge. The people did not need his clothing; they only needed his justice (قسطه)."

"What is needed from the Imam is that when he speaks, he is truthful; when he promises, he fulfills; and when he judges, he is just. For Allah does not forbid any food or drink that is lawful (حلال); He only forbade the unlawful (الحرام), be it little or much. And Allah has said: قل من حرم زينة الله التي أخرج لعباده والطيبات من الرزق **'Say, "Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has brought forth for His servants, and the good things of [His] provision?"' (7:32)**"

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Muhammad ibn Muslim, he said: I said to Abu Ja’far (peace be upon him): "How long did Ya’qub live with Yusuf in Egypt after Allah reunited Ya’qub’s family and showed him the fulfillment of Yusuf’s true dream?" He said: "He lived for two years." I said: "Who was the Proof (الحجة) of Allah on earth in those days? Ya’qub or Yusuf?" He said: "Ya’qub was the Proof, and the sovereignty (الملك) belonged to Yusuf. When Ya’qub died, Yusuf carried Ya’qub’s bones in a coffin to the land of Sham and buried him in Jerusalem. Then Yusuf son of Ya’qub became the Proof."

**I say:** The narrations regarding his (peace be upon him) story are numerous; we have limited ourselves to those that have a connection to the noble verses, especially since most of them are not free from confusion in the text or weakness in the chain.

Among what has been mentioned in some of them is that Allah, the Glorified, placed the prophethood from the House of Ya’qub in the loins of Levi (لاوي), who was the one who prevented his brothers from killing Yusuf when he said: لا تقتلوا يوسف وألقوه في غيابة الجب **"Do not kill Yusuf, but cast him into the depths of the pit" (12:10)** and who said to his brothers when Yusuf took his brother on the accusation of theft: فلن أبرح الأرض حتى يأذن لي أبي أو يحكم الله لي وهو خير الحاكمين **"I will never leave this land until my father permits me, or Allah judges for me, and He is the best of judges." (12:80)** So Allah rewarded him for that.

Among what has been mentioned in several of them is that Yusuf (peace be upon him) married the wife of the *Aziz*, who was the one who enticed him against his soul. This occurred after the *Aziz* died during those years of famine. It is not unlikely that this was a reward from Him, the Exalted, for her when she testified to Yusuf’s truthfulness by saying: الآن حصحص الحق أنا راودته عن نفسه وإنه لمن الصادقين **"Now the truth has become known. It was I who enticed him against his soul, and he is indeed truthful." (12:51)**—if the tradition is authentic.

## ( A Discussion on the Story of Yusuf in Sections )

**1 - His Story in the Quran:** He is Yusuf the Prophet, son of Ya’qub, son of Ishaq, son of Ibrahim the Friend (الخليل). He was one of the twelve sons of Ya’qub and the youngest of his brothers except for his brother Binyamin. Allah, the Glorified, willed to complete His blessing upon him with knowledge, judgment, might, and sovereignty, and to raise the status of the House of Ya’qub through him. So He gave him glad tidings while he was young through a dream he saw: eleven stars and the sun and the moon prostrating to him. He mentioned this to his father, who advised him not to recount his dream to his brothers lest they envy him. Then he interpreted his dream: that Allah would choose him, teach him the interpretation of dreams, and complete His blessing upon him and upon the House of Ya’qub as He had completed it upon his two fathers before, Ibrahim and Ishaq.

This dream remained before Yusuf’s eyes, captivating his heart. His soul never ceased yearning for the love of his Lord and being enamored with Him, given his nobility of soul, purity of spirit, and praiseworthy characteristics. He possessed a wondrous beauty that dazzled the sight and bewildered the minds.

Ya’qub loved him with an intense love because of the wondrous beauty he witnessed in him and the purity of heart he discerned in him. He would not part from him for even an hour. This weighed heavily upon his older brothers, and their envy of him intensified until they gathered and conspired regarding him. Some suggested killing him, while others said: "Cast him into some land so that your father’s attention may be exclusively yours, and you may be righteous people thereafter." Then their opinion settled on what one of them suggested: to cast him into the depths of the pit (غيابة الجب) so that some caravan might pick him up. They resolved upon this.

They met their father and spoke to him to send Yusuf with them the next day to frolic and play, promising to guard him. Ya’qub did not agree and excused himself by saying he feared a wolf might eat him. They did not cease entreating him until they satisfied him. They took him from him and went with him to their sheep’s pastures in the wilderness, where they cast him into a pit after stripping him of his shirt.

Then they came to their father weeping, with his shirt stained with false blood. They informed him that they had gone racing and left Yusuf with their belongings, and a wolf had eaten him—and this was his shirt stained with his blood.

Ya’qub wept and said: بل سولت لكم أنفسكم أمرا فصبر جميل والله المستعان على ما تصفون **"No, your souls have conjured up something for you. So [my recourse is] a goodly patience (صبر جميل), and it is Allah whose help is to be sought against what you describe." (12:18)** He only said this through a Divine discernment (تفرس إلهي) cast into his heart. Ya’qub did not cease mentioning Yusuf and weeping for him, finding no solace in anything, until his eyes turned white from grief while he was suppressed (كظيم).

His sons went to watch the pit until a caravan arrived. They sent their water-drawer to fetch water; he let down his bucket, and Yusuf clung to it and came out. They rejoiced at him. Then the sons of Ya’qub approached them and claimed he was a slave of theirs, then bargained with them until they sold him for a paltry price—a few dirhams.

The caravan traveled with him to Egypt and offered him for sale. The *Aziz* of Egypt bought him, brought him into his house, and said to his wife: "Make his stay honorable; perhaps he may be of use to us, or we may adopt him as a son." This was because of the signs of majesty and purity of spirit he witnessed in his face, despite his wondrous beauty. So Yusuf settled in the house of the *Aziz* in honor and the most pleasant life. This was the first manifestation of Allah’s subtle care for Yusuf and His mighty guardianship over him. His brothers had sought, by casting him into the pit and selling him to the caravan, to extinguish his memory and deprive him of the honor of life in his father’s house. As for the memory, his father never forgot him; as for the privilege of life, Allah, the Glorified, replaced for him the tent and the desert life with a royal palace and a sophisticated urban life. Thus, Allah raised his status through the very means by which they intended to lower and debase him. In this manner, Allah’s handiwork proceeded with him as he moved through the course of events.

Yusuf lived in the house of the *Aziz* in the most pleasant life until he grew up and reached his maturity (أشده). His soul continued to flourish and his heart to become pure, and he occupied himself with his Lord until he was enamored with His love and became sincere (أخلص) to Him. He had no concern but Him. So Allah chose him, purified him for Himself, and gave him judgment and knowledge; thus does He reward the doers of good (المحسنين).

The wife of the *Aziz* fell in love with him, and her love for him consumed her heart until she enticed him against his soul. She locked the doors and called him to herself, saying: "Come hither!" (هيت لك). Yusuf refused and sought refuge in Divine protection (عصمة إلهية) and said: معاذ الله إنه ربي أحسن مثواي إنه لا يفلح الظالمون **"God forbid! He is indeed my Lord; He has given me a goodly abode. Indeed the wrongdoers (الظالمون) — *those who overstep the bounds of truth and justice* — will not prosper." (12:23)** They raced to the door; she grabbed him and tore his shirt from behind. They found her master at the door. She accused Yusuf of wanting evil with her, and Yusuf denied it. However, Divine care reached him, and a babe there in the cradle testified to his innocence, so Allah cleared him.

Then he was tried by the love of the women of Egypt and their enticements. The matter of the wife of the *Aziz* became widespread until it led to his entry into prison. The wife of the *Aziz* sought by this to discipline him so he would comply with what she wanted, and the *Aziz* sought to silence the spreading rumors that were destroying the honor of his house and tarnishing his good name.

Yusuf entered the prison, and two youths of the King entered with him. One of them mentioned he saw in his dream that he was pressing wine, and the other saw he was carrying bread on his head from which birds were eating. They asked him to interpret their dreams. He interpreted the first’s dream that he would go out and become the King’s cupbearer, and the second’s dream that he would be crucified and birds would eat from his head. It was as he said. Yusuf said to the one he thought would be saved: اذكرني عند ربك فأنساه الشيطان ذكر ربه فلبث في السجن بضع سنين **"Mention me to your master." But Satan caused him to forget mentioning him to his master. So he remained in the prison for several years. (12:42)**

After several years, the King saw a dream that terrified him. He mentioned it to his nobles and said: إني أرى سبع بقرات سمان يأكلهن سبع عجاف وسبع سنبلات خضر وأخر يابسات يا أيها الملأ أفتوني في رؤياي إن كنتم للرؤيا تعبرون قالوا أضغاث أحلام وما نحن بتأويل الأحلام بعالمين **"‘I saw [in a dream] seven fat cows being eaten by seven lean ones, and seven green ears and [seven] others that were dry. O seniors! Give me your opinion about my dream, if you can interpret dreams.’ They said, ‘[These are] confused nightmares, and we do not know the interpretation of nightmares.’" (12:43-44)** At that, the cupbearer remembered Yusuf and his interpretation of his dream. He mentioned this to the King and asked permission to return to the prison and consult Yusuf regarding the dream. The King permitted him and sent him to him.

When he came to him and consulted him regarding the dream, mentioning that the people were waiting for him to reveal its meaning, Yusuf said: تزرعون سبع سنين دأبا فما حصدتم فذروه في سنبله إلا قليلا مما تأكلون ثم يأتي من بعد ذلك سبع شداد يأكلن ما قدمتم لهن إلا قليلا مما تحصنون ثم يأتي من بعد ذلك عام فيه يغاث الناس وفيه يعصرون **"‘You will sow for seven years consecutively. Then leave what you harvest in its ear, except a little that you eat. Then after that there will come seven hard [years] which will eat up what you have set aside for them—all except a little which you preserve [as seed]. Then after that there will come a year in which the people will be helped and in which they will press [olives and grapes].’" (12:47-49)**

When the King heard Yusuf’s verdict, it pleased him. He ordered his release and his presence. When the messenger came to execute the King’s command, Yusuf refused to leave or appear until the King investigated what had happened between him and the women and judged between him and them. When he summoned them and spoke to them regarding him, they agreed on his innocence from all he was accused of and said: "God forbid! We know no evil of him." The wife of the *Aziz* said: الآن حصحص الحق أنا راودته عن نفسه وإنه لمن الصادقين **"Now the truth has become known. It was I who enticed him against his soul, and he is indeed truthful." (12:51)** The King was greatly impressed by his knowledge, judgment, integrity, and trustworthiness. He ordered his release and his presence with honor, saying: ائتوني به أستخلصه لنفسي فلما **"‘Bring him to me, so that I may take him for my own service.’ And when"** he arrived and كلمه قال إنك اليوم لدينا مكين أمين **"he had spoken with him, he said, ‘Indeed today you are with us established and trusted.’" (12:54)** He had been refined by the best refinement and tested by the most precise testing.

Yusuf said: اجعلني على خزائن الأرض **"Put me in charge of the storehouses of the land"**—the land of Egypt— إني حفيظ عليم **"for I am indeed a knowing guardian" (12:55)** so that I may prepare the state during these seven fertile years that are coming upon the people to save them from the seven years of famine that threaten them. The King granted him that. Yusuf took charge of the matter, ordering the excellence and increase of cultivation, the collection of food and provisions, and their preservation in storehouses with prudence and management. When the years of famine struck them, he distributed the provisions among them and divided the food, until Allah saved them from starvation through that. During these years, Yusuf was established in the position of the *Aziz* of Egypt and took over the royal throne. Thus, the prison was the path he trod to the throne of might and sovereignty by Allah’s permission, though they had intended by it to extinguish his memory, make people forget him, and hide him from their eyes.

In some of those years of famine, Yusuf’s brothers entered upon him to obtain food. He recognized them while they did not recognize him. He inquired about their state and themselves; they mentioned they were the sons of Ya’qub and that they were eleven brothers, the youngest of whom was with their father, who was comforted by him and would never let him part from him. Yusuf expressed a desire to see him to know why his father favored him so. He commanded them to bring him if they returned to him a second time for provisions, and he increased their honor and fulfilled their measure. They gave him their pledge for that. He ordered his servants to put their merchandise back into their saddlebags, so they might recognize it when they returned to their family and perhaps return.

When they returned to their father, they told him what had happened between them and the *Aziz* of Egypt, and that he had denied them the measure unless they returned to him with their brother Binyamin. Their father refused. When they opened their baggage, they found their merchandise returned to them. They appealed to their father, mentioning this and insisting on sending Binyamin with them to Egypt. He refused until he agreed after taking from them a pledge by Allah that they would bring him back unless they were overwhelmed.

Then they prepared a second time and traveled to Egypt with Binyamin. When they entered upon Yusuf, he gave a place to his brother with himself and informed him of his identity, saying: "I am indeed your brother," and told him that he wished to keep him with him, so he should not be distressed by the plot he would witness.

فلما جهزهم بجهازهم جعل السقاية في رحل أخيه ثم أذن مؤذن أيتها العير إنكم لسارقون قالوا وأقبلوا عليهم ما ذا تفقدون قالوا نفقد صواع الملك ولمن جاء به حمل بعير وأنا به زعيم قالوا تالله لقد علمتم ما جئنا لنفسد في الأرض وما كنا سارقين قالوا فما جزاؤه إن كنتم كاذبين قالوا جزاؤه من وجد في رحله فهو جزاؤه **"When he had furnished them with their provisions, he put the drinking-cup into his brother’s saddlebag. Then a herald shouted, ‘O [men of the] caravan! You are indeed thieves!’ They said, as they turned toward them, ‘What is it that you are missing?’ They said, ‘We miss the King’s goblet, and whoever brings it shall have a camel-load [of grain], and I guarantee that.’ They said, ‘By Allah, you certainly know that we did not come to cause corruption in the land, and we have not been thieves.’ They said, ‘Then what shall be its penalty, if you are liars?’ They said, ‘Its penalty is that he in whose saddlebag it is found shall himself be its penalty.’" (12:70-75)** Thus do we punish the thief among us. فبدأ بأوعيتهم قبل وعاء أخيه ثم استخرجها من وعاء أخيه **"So he began with their sacks before his brother’s sack, then he took it out from his brother’s sack." (12:76)** Then he ordered his arrest and enslaved him thereby.

His brothers appealed to him for his release, even asking him to take one of them in his place out of mercy for their aged father. It was of no avail. They returned to their father in despair, except that their eldest said to them: ألم تعلموا أن أباكم قد أخذ عليكم موثقا من الله ومن قبل ما فرطتم في يوسف فلن أبرح الأرض حتى يأذن لي أبي أو يحكم الله لي وهو خير الحاكمين **"‘Do you not know that your father has taken a pledge from you by Allah, and before this you failed regarding Yusuf? So I will never leave this land until my father permits me, or Allah judges for me, and He is the best of judges.’" (12:80)** So he remained in Egypt and they traveled.

When they returned to their father and recounted the stories, he said: بل سولت لكم أنفسكم أمرا فصبر جميل عسى الله أن يأتيني بهم جميعا **"No, your souls have conjured up something for you. So [my recourse is] a goodly patience. Maybe Allah will bring them all back to me." (12:83)** Then he turned away from them وقال يا أسفى على يوسف وابيضت عيناه من الحزن فهو كظيم **"and said, ‘Alas for Yusuf!’ And his eyes turned white with grief, and he was suppressed." (12:84)** When they blamed him for his long grief and longing for Yusuf, he said: إنما أشكو بثي وحزني إلى الله وأعلم من الله ما لا تعلمون **"I only complain of my grief and sorrow to Allah, and I know from Allah what you do not know." (12:86)** Then he said to them: يا بني اذهبوا فتحسسوا من يوسف وأخيه ولا تيأسوا من روح الله **"‘O my sons! Go and inquire about Yusuf and his brother, and do not despair of Allah’s comfort.’" (12:87)** For I hope that you will find them.

A group of them traveled to Egypt and sought permission to see Yusuf. When they appeared before him, they pleaded with him and sought his mercy for themselves, their family, and their brother whom he had enslaved, saying: يا أيها العزيز مسنا وأهلنا الضر **"O *Aziz*! Distress has visited us and our family"** through famine and drought, وجئنا ببضاعة مزجاة فأوف لنا الكيل وتصدق علينا **"and we have brought a meager sum. So dispense the full measure to us and be charitable to us"** with our brother whom you have taken as a slave, إن الله يجزي المتصدقين **"Indeed Allah rewards the charitable." (12:88)**

At that point, His word, the Exalted, came true: that He would surely grant Yusuf might despite their humiliation of him, and He would surely raise his status and the status of his brother, and He would surely lower the envious transgressors. Yusuf wished to make himself known to them and said to them: هل علمتم ما فعلتم بيوسف وأخيه إذ أنتم جاهلون قالوا أإنك لأنت يوسف قال أنا يوسف وهذا أخي قد من الله علينا إنه من يتق ويصبر فإن الله لا يضيع أجر المحسنين قالوا تالله لقد آثرك الله علينا وإن كنا لخاطئين **"‘Did you know what you did to Yusuf and his brother, when you were ignorant?’ They said, ‘Are you really Yusuf?’ He said, ‘I am Yusuf, and this is my brother. Allah has certainly been gracious to us. Indeed whoever is God-conscious and patient—Allah does not let the reward of the virtuous go to waste.’ They said, ‘By Allah, Allah has certainly preferred you over us, and we have indeed been erring.’" (12:89-91)** So they confessed their sin and testified that the matter belongs to Allah—He grants might to whom He wills and humbles whom He wills, and the end is for the God-conscious, and Allah is with the patient. Yusuf met them with forgiveness and seeking pardon, saying: لا تثريب عليكم اليوم يغفر الله لكم **"There shall be no reproach on you today. Allah will forgive you" (12:92)** and he drew them close to him and increased their honor.

Then he commanded them to return to their families and take his shirt and cast it upon his father’s face so he would regain his sight. They prepared for the journey. When the caravan departed, Ya’qub said to those of his sons present with him: إني لأجد ريح يوسف لو لا أن تفندون **"I feel the scent of Yusuf, if you will not think me doting."** Those of his sons present said: تالله إنك لفي ضلالك القديم **"By Allah, you are indeed in your old error."** When the bearer of good news arrived, he cast the shirt upon his face and he regained his sight. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, restored his sight to him through the very thing that had taken it away—the shirt. Ya’qub said to his sons: ألم أقل لكم إني أعلم من الله ما لا تعلمون قالوا يا أبانا استغفر لنا ذنوبنا إنا كنا خاطئين قال سوف أستغفر لكم ربي إنه هو الغفور الرحيم **"‘Did I not tell you, "I know from Allah what you do not know"?’ They said, ‘O father! Plead [with Allah] for the forgiveness of our sins! Indeed we have been erring.’ He said, ‘I shall plead with my Lord to forgive you. Indeed He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful.’" (12:96-98)**

Then they prepared for the journey to Yusuf, and Yusuf received them and drew his parents to himself, granted them security, and brought them into the royal abode. He raised his parents upon the throne, and they fell down before him in prostration—Ya’qub, his wife, and eleven of his sons. (**قالَ**) Yusuf (**يا أَبَتِ هذا تَأْوِيلُ رُءْيايَ مِنْ قَبْلُ قَدْ جَعَلَها رَبِّي حَقًّا**) — *“O my father! This is the fulfillment of my dream of old. My Lord has certainly made it come true.”* (12:100). Then he thanked Allah for His subtle grace (لطيف صنعه) in averting great calamities from him and bestowing upon him sovereignty and knowledge.

The family of Ya’qub remained in Egypt. The people of Egypt loved Yusuf with an intense love due to the excellence of his favor upon them and his beautiful conduct (حسن بلائه) among them. He used to call them to the religion of monotheism (دين التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and the creed of his forefathers, Ibrahim, Ishaq, and Ya’qub, peace be upon them (عليه‌السلام), as mentioned in the story of the prison and in Surah al-Mu’min.

**2 – That for which Allah praised him and his spiritual station:**
He (peace be upon him) was among the purified (المخلصين), he was a man of truth (صديقا), and he was among the doers of good (المحسنين). Allah granted him judgment (حكما) and knowledge (علما), and taught him the interpretation of dreams (تأويل الأحاديث). Allah chose him (اجتباه), perfected His favor upon him, and joined him with the righteous (الصالحين) (Surah Yusuf). He praised him with that which He praised the family of Nuh and Ibrahim (peace be upon them) among the prophets, and mentioned him among them (Surah al-An’am).

**3 – His story in the current Torah:**
The Torah says: And (1) the sons of Ya’qub were twelve: the sons of Leah: Reuben, Ya’qub’s firstborn, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun. The two sons of Rachel: Yusuf and Benjamin. The two sons of Bilhah, Rachel’s maidservant: Dan and Naphtali. And the two sons of Zilpah, Leah’s maidservant: Gad and Asher. These are the sons of Ya’qub who were born in Paddan-aram.

It says (1) that Yusuf, being seventeen years old, was feeding the flock with his brothers; and the lad was with the sons of Bilhah and the sons of Zilpah, his father’s wives; and Yusuf brought their evil report to their father. Now Israel loved Yusuf more than all his children, because he was the son of his old age; and he made him a coat of many colors. And when his brothers saw that their father loved him more than all his brothers, they hated him, and could not speak peaceably to him.

And Yusuf dreamed a dream, and he told it to his brothers; and they hated him yet the more. And he said to them: "Hear, I pray you, this dream which I have dreamed: For, behold, we were binding sheaves in the field, and, lo, my sheaf arose, and also stood upright; and, behold, your sheaves stood round about, and made obeisance to my sheaf." And his brothers said to him: "Shalt thou indeed reign over us? Or shalt thou indeed have dominion over us?" And they hated him yet the more for his dreams, and for his words.

And he dreamed yet another dream, and told it to his brothers, and said: "Behold, I have dreamed a dream more; and, behold, the sun and the moon and the eleven stars made obeisance to me." And he told it to his father, and to his brothers; and his father rebuked him, and said to him: "What is this dream that thou hast dreamed? Shall I and thy mother and thy brothers indeed come to bow down ourselves to thee to the earth?" And his brothers envied him; but his father observed the saying.

And his brothers went to feed their father’s flock in Shechem. And Israel said to Yusuf: "Do not thy brothers feed the flock in Shechem? Come, and I will send thee unto them." And he said to him: "Here am I." And he said to him: "Go, I pray thee, see whether it be well with thy brothers, and well with the flocks; and bring me word again." So he sent him out of the vale of Hebron, and he came to Shechem. And a certain man found him, and, behold, he was wandering in the field; and the man asked him, saying: "What seekest thou?" And he said: "I seek my brothers; tell me, I pray thee, where they feed their flocks." And the man said: "They are departed hence; for I heard them say, 'Let us go to Dothan.'" And Yusuf went after his brothers, and found them in Dothan.

And when they saw him afar off, even before he came near unto them, they conspired against him to slay him. And they said one to another: "Behold, this dreamer cometh. Come now therefore, and let us slay him, and cast him into some pit, and we will say, 'Some evil beast hath devoured him'; and we shall see what will become of his dreams." And Reuben heard it, and he delivered him out of their hands; and said: "Let us not kill him." And Reuben said to them: "Shed no blood, but cast him into this pit that is in the wilderness, and lay no hand upon him"; that he might rid him out of their hands, to deliver him to his father again. And it came to pass, when Yusuf was come unto his brothers, that they stripped Yusuf out of his coat, his coat of many colors that was on him; and they took him, and cast him into a pit; and the pit was empty, there was no water in it.

And they sat down to eat bread; and they lifted up their eyes and looked, and, behold, a company of Ishmaelites came from Gilead with their camels bearing spicery and balm and myrrh, going to carry it down to Egypt. And Judah said unto his brothers: "What profit is it if we slay our brother, and conceal his blood? Come, and let us sell him to the Ishmaelites, and let not our hand be upon him; for he is our brother and our flesh." And his brothers were content.

Then there passed by Midianites merchantmen; and they drew and lifted up Yusuf out of the pit, and sold Yusuf to the Ishmaelites for twenty pieces of silver; and they brought Yusuf into Egypt. And Reuben returned unto the pit; and, behold, Yusuf was not in the pit; and he rent his clothes. And he returned unto his brothers, and said: "The child is not; and I, whither shall I go?"

And they took Yusuf’s coat, and killed a kid of the goats, and dipped the coat in the blood; and they sent the coat of many colors, and they brought it to their father; and said: "This have we found; know now whether it be thy son’s coat or no." And he knew it, and said: "It is my son’s coat; an evil beast hath devoured him; Yusuf is without doubt rent in pieces." And Ya’qub rent his clothes, and put sackcloth upon his loins, and mourned for his son many days. And all his sons and all his daughters rose up to comfort him; but he refused to be comforted; and he said: "For I will go down into the grave unto my son mourning." Thus his father wept for him.

The Torah says (1): "And Yusuf was brought down to Egypt; and Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh, captain of the guard, an Egyptian, bought him of the hands of the Ishmaelites, which had brought him down thither. And the Lord was with Yusuf, and he was a prosperous man; and he was in the house of his master the Egyptian.

And his master saw that the Lord was with him, and that the Lord made all that he did to prosper in his hand. And Yusuf found grace in his sight, and he served him; and he made him overseer over his house, and all that he had he put into his hand. And it came to pass from the time that he had made him overseer in his house, and over all that he had, that the Lord blessed the Egyptian’s house for Yusuf’s sake; and the blessing of the Lord was upon all that he had in the house, and in the field. And he left all that he had in Yusuf’s hand; and he knew not ought he had, save the bread which he did eat. And Yusuf was a goodly person, and well favored.

And it came to pass after these things, that his master’s wife cast her eyes upon Yusuf; and she said: 'Lie with me.' But he refused, and said unto his master’s wife: 'Behold, my master wotteth not what is with me in the house, and he hath committed all that he hath to my hand; there is none greater in this house than I; neither hath he kept back any thing from me but thee, because thou art his wife: how then can I do this great wickedness, and sin against God?' And it came to pass, as she spake to Yusuf day by day, that he hearkened not unto her, to lie by her, or to be with her.

And it came to pass about this time, that Yusuf went into the house to do his business; and there was none of the men of the house there within. And she caught him by his garment, saying: 'Lie with me'; and he left his garment in her hand, and fled, and got him out. And it came to pass, when she saw that he had left his garment in her hand, and was fled forth, that she called unto the men of her house, and spake unto them, saying: 'See, he hath brought in an Hebrew unto us to mock us; he came in unto me to lie with me, and I cried with a loud voice: and it came to pass, when he heard that I lifted up my voice and cried, that he left his garment with me, and fled, and got him out.'

And she laid up his garment by her, until his lord came home. And she spake unto him according to these words, saying: 'The Hebrew servant, which thou hast brought unto us, came in unto me to mock me: and it came to pass, as I lifted up my voice and cried, that he left his garment with me, and fled out.'

And it came to pass, when his master heard the words of his wife, which she spake unto him, saying: 'After this manner did thy servant to me'; that his wrath was kindled. And Yusuf’s master took him, and put him into the prison, a place where the king’s prisoners were bound; and he was there in the prison.

But the Lord was with Yusuf, and shewed him mercy, and gave him favor in the sight of the keeper of the prison. And the keeper of the prison committed to Yusuf’s hand all the prisoners that were in the prison; and whatsoever they did there, he was the doer of it. The keeper of the prison looked not to any thing that was under his hand; because the Lord was with him, and that which he did, the Lord made it to prosper."

Then (1) the Torah relates the story of the two companions of the prison and their dreams, and the dream of the Pharaoh of Egypt. The summary is that they were the chief of the butlers of Pharaoh and the chief of the bakers; they had offended, so Pharaoh imprisoned them in the prison of the captain of the guard with Yusuf. The chief butler saw in his dream that he was pressing wine, and the other saw that birds were eating from food he carried on his head. They sought a ruling from Yusuf, so he interpreted the dream of the first as his return to Pharaoh’s service in his previous job, and the second as his crucifixion and the birds eating from his flesh. He asked the butler to mention him to Pharaoh that he might be released from prison, but Satan made him forget that.

Then after two years, Pharaoh saw in his dream seven fat kine, well favored, coming out of the river; and seven lean kine, ill favored, stood on the bank; and the lean ones ate the fat ones. Pharaoh awoke, then slept and saw seven ears of corn, green, good, and fat; and seven thin ears, blasted with the east wind, springing up after them; and the thin ones ate the fat ones. This terrified Pharaoh, and he gathered the magicians of Egypt and its wise men and recounted his dream to them, but they were unable to interpret it.

At that point, the chief butler remembered Yusuf and mentioned him to Pharaoh, mentioning what he had witnessed of his wondrous interpretation of dreams. Pharaoh ordered him to be brought. When he was brought in, he spoke to him and sought his ruling regarding what he had seen in his dream repeatedly. Yusuf said to Pharaoh: "The dream of Pharaoh is one; God hath shewed Pharaoh what He is about to do. The seven good kine are seven years; and the seven good ears are seven years: the dream is one. And the seven thin and ill favored kine that came up after them are seven years; and the seven empty ears blasted with the east wind shall be seven years of famine.

This is the thing which I have spoken unto Pharaoh: What God is about to do He sheweth unto Pharaoh. Behold, there come seven years of great plenty throughout all the land of Egypt: and there shall arise after them seven years of famine; and all the plenty shall be forgotten in the land of Egypt; and the famine shall consume the land; and the plenty shall not be known in the land by reason of that famine following; for it shall be very grievous. And for that the dream was doubled unto Pharaoh twice; it is because the thing is established by God, and God will shortly bring it to pass.

Now therefore let Pharaoh look out a man discreet and wise, and set him over the land of Egypt. Let Pharaoh do this, and let him appoint officers over the land, and take up the fifth part of the land of Egypt in the seven plenteous years. And let them gather all the food of those good years that come, and lay up corn under the hand of Pharaoh, and let them keep food in the cities. And that food shall be for store to the land against the seven years of famine, which shall be in the land of Egypt; that the land perish not through the famine."

The Torah says in summary that Pharaoh approved of Yusuf’s words and his interpretation and honored him. He gave him the governorship of the kingdom in all its affairs, took off his ring and put it on Yusuf’s hand, arrayed him in vestures of fine linen, put a gold chain about his neck, and made him to ride in his second chariot; and they cried before him: "Bow the knee!" Yusuf began managing affairs in the years of plenty, and then in the years of drought, he managed them excellently.

Then (1) the Torah says in summary that when the famine spread to the land of Canaan, Ya’qub ordered his sons to go down to Egypt to get food. They traveled and entered upon Yusuf; he recognized them, but they did not recognize him. He spoke to them harshly and asked them: "Whence come ye?" They said: "From the land of Canaan to buy food." Yusuf said: "Nay, but spies ye are; to see the nakedness of the land ye are come." They said: "We are all one man’s sons in Canaan; we were twelve brothers; one is no more, and the youngest is this day with our father, and the rest are before thee. We are all true men; we know no corruption or evil."

Yusuf said: "Nay, by the life of Pharaoh, we see you as spies; and ye shall not go forth hence, except your youngest brother come hither, that we may believe what ye claim." He ordered them to be imprisoned for three days, then he brought them forth and took Simeon from among them and bound him before their eyes, and permitted them to return to Canaan and bring their youngest brother.

Then he ordered their vessels to be filled with corn and to restore every man’s money into his sack. They did so and returned to their father and recounted the stories to him. Ya’qub refused to send Benjamin with them and said: "Me have ye bereaved of my children: Yusuf is not, and Simeon is not, and ye will take Benjamin away; this shall never be." And he said: "Ye have dealt ill with me in telling the man whether ye had yet a brother." They said: "The man asked us straitly of our state, and of our kindred, saying, 'Is your father yet alive? Have ye another brother?' and we told him according to the tenor of these words: could we certainly know that he would say, 'Bring your brother down'?"

Ya’qub continued to refuse until Judah gave him a pledge (الموثق) that he would return Benjamin to him. So he permitted them to go with him, and ordered them to take of the best fruits in the land as a gift to the man, and to take double money with them, including the money that was brought again in the mouth of their sacks. They did so.

When they arrived in Egypt, they met Yusuf’s steward and informed him of their need and that their money had been returned to them in their bags. They presented their gift to him, and he welcomed them and honored them, informing them that their money was theirs. He brought Simeon the hostage out to them, then brought them in to Yusuf. They bowed down to him and presented their gift to him. He welcomed them and inquired about their state and the welfare of their father. They presented their youngest brother to him, and he honored him and prayed for him. Then he ordered food to be served; it was served for him alone, for them alone, and for the Egyptians with him alone.

Then he ordered his steward to fill their vessels with food, to put their gift therein, and to put a cup in the sack of their youngest brother. He did so. When the morning was light, the men were sent away, they and their asses.

When they were gone out of the city, and not yet far off, he said to his steward: "Up, follow after the men; and when thou dost overtake them, say unto them, 'Wherefore have ye rewarded evil for good? Have ye stolen my lord’s cup whereby he drinketh, and whereby indeed he divineth?'" They were confounded upon hearing this and said: "God forbid that thy servants should do according to this thing. Behold, the money, which we found in our sacks’ mouths, we brought again unto thee out of the land of Canaan: how then should we steal out of thy lord’s house silver or gold? With whomsoever of thy servants it be found, both let him die, and we also will be my lord’s bondmen." He agreed to the penalty they mentioned, so they hastened to their sacks, and every man took down his sack and opened it. He began to search, starting from the eldest until he ended at the youngest, and the cup was found in his sack.

When his brothers saw that, they rent their clothes and returned to the city and entered upon Yusuf. They repeated their words to him, apologizing and confessing their guilt, with signs of smallness, humiliation, and shame upon them. He said: "God forbid that I should take any but him in whose hand the cup is found; but as for you, get you up in peace unto your father."

Then Judah came near unto him and entreated him and sought his mercy, mentioning their story with their father when Yusuf ordered them to bring Benjamin. They had asked their father for that, but he refused most vehemently until Judah gave him a covenant to return Benjamin to him. He mentioned that they could not meet their father without Benjamin, and that their aged father, if he heard that from them, would die instantly. Then he asked him to take him instead of Benjamin as a slave for himself and to release Benjamin, so that the eye of their father—who found comfort in him after the loss of his brother from his mother, Yusuf—might be cooled.

The Torah says: Then Yusuf could not refrain himself before all them that stood by him; and he cried: "Cause every man to go out from me." And there stood no man with him, while Yusuf made himself known unto his brothers. And he wept aloud: and the Egyptians and the house of Pharaoh heard. And Yusuf said unto his brothers: "I am Yusuf; doth my father yet live?" And his brothers could not answer him; for they were troubled at his presence.
